answer:
Claim
Domain: causal inference on retention using observational product data. Claim: feature F caused retention to improve. Metric/outcome: retention over a frozen horizon, for example D30 retained or next-period active. Win condition: estimated causal effect of feature F on retention is positive, practically meaningful, and robust across identification, sensitivity, and replication checks. Guardrails: no harm to key segments, no induced selection bias, no metric gaming, no hidden retention cost such as lower activation quality or increased churn later.

Verifier Boundary
Freeze inputs: dataset snapshot, cohort definition, feature exposure definition, retention window, covariates available before exposure, exclusions, and missing-data policy. Freeze evaluator/rubric: causal estimand, model family, adjustment plan, negative controls, sensitivity thresholds, and decision boundary before looking at favorable variants. Mutable candidate: identification strategy and estimation method may be refined only under logged rules. Forbidden access: post-treatment variables, leaked future behavior, hand-picked cohorts, or outcome-informed feature definitions. Tampering rules: any cohort, metric, or covariate change after seeing effects invalidates the current ledger unless re-registered. Review authority: causal review owner or statistics lead decides whether evidence clears the gates.

Baseline / Candidate Family
Baseline: raw association between exposure to feature F and retention, plus the current retention trend without causal adjustment. Candidate family: observational causal designs such as difference-in-differences, matching/weighting, regression adjustment, instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, or panel fixed effects, depending on how exposure was assigned. The estimand must be explicit: ATE, ATT, LATE, or segment-specific effect for defined users over a defined time window.

Current Evidence
OPEN: An observational dataset can support a causal claim only if the assignment mechanism and assumptions are credible. SUPPORTED: A positive adjusted association can motivate deeper causal testing. REJECTED: Association alone proves feature F caused retention to improve. REJECTED: A benchmark lift, dashboard trend, or model coefficient is proof without identification. OPEN: The causal claim remains open until identification, negative controls, sensitivity, and replication pressure pass.

Enemy Terms
Association as causation. Confounding leak. Post-treatment adjustment. Survivorship bias. Selection into treatment. Seasonality. Regression to the mean. Product launch overlap. Segment mix shift. Outcome-window fishing. Certainty inflation.

Rejection Gates
If exposure to feature F is self-selected and no credible adjustment or quasi-experimental source isolates assignment, fail for identification-gap. If covariates include post-treatment behavior or future information, fail for confounding-leak. If negative controls or placebo outcomes show similar effects where feature F should not matter, fail. If placebo treatment dates show retention lift before exposure, fail. If sensitivity analysis shows a plausible unmeasured confounder could erase the effect, fail or REFINE. If the effect appears only in hand-picked cohorts, metrics, or windows, fail. If replication on later cohorts, holdout periods, or comparable segments does not transfer, fail. If the decision treats association, demo, citation, or dashboard lift as proof, fail.

Proof Ladder / Transfer Path
1. Define causal estimand: population, treatment, comparator, outcome, horizon, and minimum meaningful effect. 2. Establish baseline association and pre-trends. 3. Choose identification strategy based on assignment: DiD with parallel-trend evidence, RD around an eligibility threshold, IV with exclusion justification, or pre-registered adjustment if no stronger design exists. 4. Run negative controls, placebo treatment dates, placebo outcomes, and falsification checks. 5. Test sensitivity to unmeasured confounding, model specification, missingness, and segment composition. 6. Replicate on new cohorts or comparable contexts. 7. Decide only if effect size, uncertainty, guardrails, and hidden costs clear the frozen threshold.

Verdict
No, the observational dataset does not by itself prove feature F caused retention to improve. It may provide causal evidence if a credible identification strategy survives falsification, sensitivity, and replication. Until then, the correct statement is association or causal hypothesis, not proof.

Proof Ledger Decision
REFINE: Continue only after freezing the estimand and evaluator, selecting a defensible identification strategy, and running negative-control, sensitivity, and replication pressure.

Next Pressure
Write the causal estimand in one sentence, list the assumed assignment mechanism, choose the strongest available design, and define the exact effect threshold that would justify shipping or rollback.

Claim
Domain: product discovery and problem validation. Claim: this prototype proves we found the right user problem. Baseline: users' current workflow, workaround, failure mode, cost, frequency, and urgency before seeing the prototype. Candidate family: prototype concepts that may address the suspected problem. Metric/outcome: observed user behavior showing the problem is real and important, such as repeated workaround use, task abandonment, time cost, willingness to switch, or committed follow-up. Win condition: target users in real context consistently reveal the same problem independent of prototype polish, and evidence transfers beyond initial demo enthusiasm. Guardrails: avoid validating internal taste, novelty response, founder bias, or artifact quality instead of the underlying problem.

Verifier Boundary
Freeze inputs: target user segment, user context, research script, baseline workflow questions, task scenario, success criteria, and invalid-proxy exclusions. Freeze evaluator/rubric: what counts as problem evidence versus solution feedback, minimum sample/context diversity, and transfer gate. Mutable candidate: prototype form, copy, and interaction may change, but problem definition changes must be logged. Forbidden access: leading prompts, internal stakeholders as user substitutes, cherry-picked quotes, or demo-only reactions counted as proof. Tampering rules: do not rewrite the problem statement after positive prototype reactions without re-testing. Review authority: product/research owner decides whether the ledger clears validation gates.

Baseline / Candidate Family
Baseline workflow: what users do today without the prototype, including tools, manual steps, delays, abandoned attempts, social workarounds, and economic or emotional cost. Candidate family: prototype variants that express possible solutions to the suspected problem. The prototype tests solution direction and comprehension; it does not automatically prove problem importance.

Current Evidence
OPEN: Prototype reactions can support a problem hypothesis if tied to real user context and baseline behavior. SUPPORTED: Strong task resonance, concrete user stories, and observed current workaround pain are useful evidence. REJECTED: Internal taste proves the right user problem. REJECTED: Demo enthusiasm proves the problem is real. REJECTED: A polished prototype, high-fidelity interaction, or stakeholder excitement is proof of problem validation. OPEN: The claim remains open until user-context evidence, baseline workflow pressure, invalid-proxy checks, and transfer beyond the prototype pass.

Enemy Terms
Solutionism. Prototype theater. Demo enthusiasm. Internal taste. Invalid-user-proxy. Leading research. Novelty bias. Courtesy bias. No-transfer gate. Baseline skip. Confusing artifact quality with problem truth.

Rejection Gates
If evidence comes primarily from internal stakeholders, friends, investors, or non-target users, fail for invalid-user-proxy. If users only react to the prototype and do not describe or demonstrate the baseline workflow pain, fail. If the research script leads users toward the proposed solution, fail. If users praise the artifact but show no existing workaround, cost, urgency, or repeated need, fail for solutionism. If the problem appears only when the prototype is present, fail or REFINE. If enthusiasm does not transfer to behavior such as opting into follow-up, sharing real data, changing workflow, paying, or choosing it over current workaround, fail. If no test is run outside the initial prototype context, fail for no-transfer-gate.

Proof Ladder / Transfer Path
1. State the suspected user problem without naming the prototype. 2. Identify target users and invalid proxies. 3. Document baseline workflow and current workaround before showing the prototype. 4. Observe or collect concrete context evidence: frequency, severity, cost, failed alternatives, and trigger moments. 5. Show the prototype only after baseline pressure is established, then separate solution feedback from problem evidence. 6. Run transfer checks: different users, realistic context, competing alternatives, and behavior beyond praise. 7. Validate that the same problem persists across lower-fidelity or alternate solution forms, proving the problem is not just attraction to this artifact.

Verdict
No, the prototype does not prove we found the right user problem. It can provide evidence about whether a proposed solution resonates, but problem validation requires independent user-context evidence, baseline workflow pressure, and transfer beyond prototype enthusiasm.

Proof Ledger Decision
REFINE: Keep the prototype as a learning instrument, but rerun discovery around the baseline workflow and define behavior-based gates before claiming the problem is validated.

Next Pressure
Interview or observe target users before showing the prototype, capture their current workaround and cost, then test whether the same problem signal survives when the prototype polish and demo framing are removed.
